posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
2012 champagne seems okay but not incredible as far as i’m aware - compare 2008 cristal vs 2012, for example.

2012 is a really good vintage in Champagne! I actually tasted the 2012 and 2008 Cristal side by side last week, and having initially preferred the 2008 two years ago, I’m inclined to prefer the 2012 today. It’s a richer, more muscular vintage than 2008, with more complete maturity and more completely integrated acidity. The 2013 Cristal in many ways represents the best of both worlds (i.e. the improved viticulture that makes itself felt in 2012, combined with a more incisive, acid driven vintage) if you prefer racier, finer-boned vintages.

As for Dom Pérignon 2012, I didn’t taste it yet, but I would expect something quite powerful and dramatic more or less along the lines of their 2002. Let’s see!

posts.name: Tom Reddick
posts.cooked:
Note that this is always subject to change based on evolving supplies and market conditions, but the information I have is that 2012 Dom Perignon will officially hit the US in late summer/early fall, with 2008 Rose in early 2022. 2010 came in very modest supplies- hence the quick transition (and I would add that 2010 is really pretty and worth your consideration.)

I have not had the 2012 Dom yet- nor do I expect to have early access- but based on other 2012s or 2012-based wines I have had, I really like the look of the vintage. So far it strikes me as very well rounded and not necessarily having a strong personality in any one particular area. A less overtly powerful 2008 might be a good way to put it this early (though the 2012s I have had are certainly substantial, just without that massive hard wall many 2008s have right now)- and again I have few TNs to support that notion, so please take those thoughts with a big grain of salt.

William- thank you for the Cristal comments. I have not had the 2012 yet. Is your comparative specific to Cristal or based on broader experience? Just curious as we get into 2012 season with the big guns. I love 2008s and 2004s, but I am not a fan of 2002s. I certainly concede their greatness, but they are too sweet for me. Even a 2002 Dom Perignon P2 this past weekend was not to my liking- though I think it will be incredible in 15 more years.

While I try to avoid disparaging competitors, that chart is risible. 2013 is an excellent vintage, but they don’t even rate it; whereas 2017, which saw serious issues with volatile acidity especially in Pinot Noir, comes in at 95/100. Preferring 2005 to 2006 is also highly questionable.

posts.name: Andreas Scheppler
posts.cooked: I can second that, Cristal 2012 is currently a tad more exciting to drink vs the 2008.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
William- thank you for the Cristal comments. I have not had the 2012 yet. Is your comparative specific to Cristal or based on broader experience? Just curious as we get into 2012 season with the big guns. I love 2008s and 2004s, but I am not a fan of 2002s. I certainly concede their greatness, but they are too sweet for me. Even a 2002 Dom Perignon P2 this past weekend was not to my liking- though I think it will be incredible in 15 more years.

I was specifically referring to Cristal, where I think you taste the progress Roederer made in the vineyards between 2008 and 2012 (and which hasn’t abated since).

To generalize about Champagne as a whole, it’s interesting to talk about what could go wrong in both the vintages we are discussing. One could say that when 2008 isn’t brilliant, it’s because the grapes were a touch underripe, resulting in wines that are rather structurally lean and which were often generously dosed: 1996 had the same potential failing to a great extent. When 2012 isn’t successful, it’s because of wines that can be a bit overbearingly rich and heavy, especially when dosage wasn’t adjusted to the quite ripe, low yielding vintage. To me, it all comes down to farming though: if your viticulture produced grapes that were a touch underripe in 2008, it likely also produced grapes that lacked a bit of freshness in 2012 (especially if over-dosed). Whereas, by contrast, good farmers and winemakers made very good wines in both 2008 and 2012. I think we will see the same phenomenon in the 2018, 2019 and 2020 vintages in due course: rather than pardoning viticultural sins by allowing even bad growers to pick ripe grapes, climate change is actually throwing the difference between the best and the rest into heightened relief.

As for the style of 2012, if you do have a very strong preference for '08 and '04 over '02, it’s conceivable that you might want to emphasize 2013 over 2012 in your purchases.

I have the same preference for 2008 and 2004 over 2002 generally, and when I tried 2012 Cristal just at release, I didn’t think it had the same phenolic structure I loved in the 2008. Not to say it hasn’t developed — drinking Champagne at release can be deceiving IME and I haven’t had it since.

2013s have been very good so far. Including wines where it was the dominant or 50-50 base for NV. Esp Cote des Blancs.

2012 has more phenolic structure than 2008, but less malic and tartaric acid structure…

Offer just went out at 5pm today. I have a feeling Taittinger is slow rolling us with this release to keep demand high and keep their prices from dropping. It seems like cases are floating in at random intervals.

Not sure I am seeing the forest for the trees here, but are we now saying that 2008 Dom and 2008 Cristal are yesterday’s story and we are now on to 2012 ?

Jim,

I think that 2008 has firmly locked itself in as one of those rare “vintages of a decade”. I think that 2012 is another exceptional vintage, that is very high quality and worthy of attention. Sometimes in the past it might have been more common for 6-10 years between vintages of this quality, but with global warming they seem to be more frequent.

Based on what I’ve tasted I’m buying as much 2012 from producers that I like & trust. If you can find the 2012 L. Aubry fils champagne premier cru Aubry de Humbert, it’s a stellar value in the $66 range.
